revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:椿説弓張月]][[zh:椿說弓張月]]
[[File:Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki.jpg|thumb|320px|'Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki' ---- {{Smaller|Gen Tamechika drawing with a long bow. Excerpted from the reader "Chinzei Hachirō Tamechika Gaiden: Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki". Illustration by Katsushika Hokusai.|qq=}}]] {{Portal|Literature|qq=}} "'''Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki''' " (椿説弓張月, pronounced "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki"—also read as "Chinzei~" per <ref name="matsumura1952"/>) is a reader (読本) authored by [[:en:Kyokutei Bakin|Kyokutei Bakin]] with illustrations by [[:en:Katsushika Hokusai|Katsushika Hokusai]]. It was published in five parts across 29 volumes from 【Bunka 4】 (1807) to 【Bunka 8】 (1811). The editions were issued by Hiraibayashi Shōgorō and Bunkokudo Nishimura Genshiro<ref name="#1">{{Cite|和書|author=[[後藤丹治]]校注|title=椿説弓張月 上|date=1958-08|publisher=岩波書店|pages=3-54|ref=harv|qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite|和書|author=[[岡本勝]]・[[雲英末雄]]編|title=新版 近世文学研究事典|date=2006-02|publisher=おうふう|pages=115|ref=harv|qq=}}</ref>. "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" is a legendary tale of moral rectification (勧善懲悪の伝奇物語) that depicts the exploits of the famed archer warrior [[Minamoto Tamechika|Chinzei Hachirō Tamechika]] (also known simply as Tamechika) as featured in "Hōgen Monogatari", and interweaves with a secret history concerning the founding of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|qid=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}}. Along with "Nansō Satomi Hakkenden", it is regarded as one of Bakin’s representative works. == Overview == "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" marks Bakin’s inaugural foray into the genre of historical biography readers (史伝物読本)<ref name=":0"/>, and its narrative can be divided into two segments: one recounting Japanese legends and the other detailing Ryukyuan tales<ref name=":2">{{Cite|和書|author=[[後藤丹治]]校注|title=椿説弓張月 上|date=1958-08|publisher=岩波書店|pages=3-54|ref=harv|qq=}}</ref>. The first and second parts—comprising the prelude and subsequent chapters—depict the heroic deeds of Tamechika almost faithfully as in "Hōgen Monogatari", while the later sections (the sequel, "Shūi", and the fragmentary "Zanpen") narrate how, after fleeing to Ryukyu, Tamechika re-established the Ryukyu Kingdom (according to legend, Tamechika escaped to Ryukyu and his son later became the first Ryukyuan king, {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|qid=Q1059918|lt=Shunten|1=Shunten}})<ref>Official histories such as "Chūzan Seikan", "Omorosaushi", and "Chinzei Ryūkyūki" state that Tamechika fled to what is now {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} and that his son became the progenitor of the Ryukyuan royal family.</ref>. The synopsis is as follows: Tamechika, a master archer who journeyed down to Kyushu, becomes the son-in-law of Shiranuin, daughter of the Aso region, but is defeated in the 【Hōgen Rebellion】 and exiled to Ōshima<ref name=":0"/>. After escaping from Ōshima, he raises an army; however, amid a violent sea storm, he is shipwrecked and drifts to Ryukyu<ref name=":0"/>. In Ryukyu, Princess Chūfu of King Shōnei conspires with Riyū and Moyun to depose Princess Nei, the first daughter of King Shōnei<ref name=":0"/>. Tamechika rescues Princess Nei and brings stability to Ryukyu<ref name=":0"/>. Celebrated as one of the tragic heroes in Japanese history, the latter part of the work—which fuses his heroic wanderings with legends pertaining to the founding of the Ryukyu Kingdom—was acclaimed for its grand scale and dramatic narrative. === Brief History === The "Zenpen" (first part) was published in 【Bunka 4】 (1807), and over the subsequent four years, the "Kōpen" (second part), "Zokui", "Shūi", and "Zanpen" were released, culminating in a five-part, 29-volume series. Initially, the work was intended to be completed in 12 volumes covering just the first and second parts<ref name=":1"/><ref name="#1"/>, but overwhelming popular response prompted Bakin to extend and repeatedly postpone its Completion tier 3]]: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" (Iwanami Bunko, Upper/Middle/Lower volumes). (Lacking illustrations.) Reprinted several times. * Edited by [[後藤丹治]]: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki (Upper and Lower)" published by Iwanami Shoten (based on the first edition owned by Suzuki Shigezo, including all illustrations). === Title === The full title is inscribed as "鎮西八郎為朝外伝 椿説弓張月". Here, "椿説" (read "Chinsetsu") means "an unusual or rare theory" (akin to "珍説") and also resonates with "鎮西" (referring to Tamechika, known as "Chinzei Hachirō Tamechika")<ref name=":2" />; "弓張月" is so named because the protagonist is renowned for his archery prowess<ref name=":2" />; "外伝" denotes that this is an unofficial biography, deliberately deviating from strict historical record<ref name=":2" />. == Main Characters == * '''Minamoto Tamechika''' {{Smaller|（みなもとの ためとも）|qq=}}: The eighth son of Minamoto Tameyoshi and a celebrated master archer. * '''Shiranuin-hime''' {{Smaller|（しらぬい ひめ）|qq=}}: Tamechika’s primary wife, in whom the spirit of the legendary princess is incarnated. * '''King Shōnei''' {{Smaller|（しょうねい おう）|qq=}}: The king of Ryukyu. * '''Princess Nei''' {{Smaller|（ねい わんにょ）|qq=}}: The first princess of King Shōnei. * '''Princess Shiranuin''' {{Smaller|（しらぬい わんにょ）|qq=}}: The embodiment of Shiranuin-hime’s spirit in the form of Princess Nei. * '''Hacchō Tsubute no Kiheiji''' {{Smaller|（はっちょう つぶての きへいじ）|qq=}}: Tamechika’s loyal retainer and an expert in stone-throwing (also known as Inji), who also raises Shuntenmaru. * '''Shuntenmaru''' {{Smaller|（すてまる）|qq=}}: The legitimate son of Tamechika and Shiranuin-hime, who defeats Moyun and becomes King Shunten. * '''Tsurukame''' {{Smaller|（つる・かめ）|qq=}}: The two sons of Mou Kokutei, vassals of the Ryukyu Kingdom. * '''Agō''' {{Smaller|（くまきみ）|qq=}}: A famed shrine maiden (巫女) of the Ryukyu Kingdom who colludes in the conspiracy led by Riyū. * '''Moyun''' {{Smaller|（もう うん）|qq=}}: A mysterious monk emerging from a dragon mound, wielding supernatural powers to command demonic beasts (禍). * '''Sutoku-in''' {{Smaller|（しゅとく いん）|qq=}}: The retired emperor (上皇) once served by Tamechika, who appears as an avenging spirit in times of crisis. == Sources == The sources for "Yumihari-zuki" are numerous; here are a few representative examples: ; Noh Song "Amajin" : Employed during the early conception of "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki", it contributed significantly to the work’s framework<ref>{{Cite journal|author=大高洋司|date=2004|title=『椿説弓張月』の構想と謡曲「海人」|journal=近世文藝|volume=79|pages=17-28|publisher=日本近世文学会|doi=10.20815/kinseibungei.79.0_17|qq=}}</ref> ; "Hōgen Monogatari" <ref name=":2" /> : The source text for the initial section. Note that Bakin adopted the version compiled and published at Mito's Shōkokan during the Genroku period<ref name=":2" />, and he also partially used the Kansai reader "Hōgen Heiji Tōzu-kai"<ref>{{Cite journal|author=三宅宏幸|date=2011-03|title=『椿説弓張月』典拠小考|journal=同志社国文学|volume=74|pages=45-56|publisher=同志社大学国文学会|doi=10.14988/pa.2017.0000012691|qq=}}</ref>. ; Sato Yukinobu’s "Izu-koku Kaishima Fudoki" <ref name=":2" /> : Written in 【Tenmei 2】 (1782), a geographical record of the Izu Islands. A manuscript bearing Bakin's library seal remains<ref name=":2" />. ; "Suikō Kōden" by the descendants of Song refugees <ref name=":2" /> : Serves as a later source, borrowing characters and motifs. ; Xu Baoguang’s "Chūzan Den Shinkiroku" <ref name=":2" /> : A six-volume work utilized for names, events, and details concerning Ryukyu. == Contemporary Evaluation == The work received overwhelming support from the common people and was a commercial success, firmly establishing Bakin’s reputation as an author of readers. It inspired numerous omnibus editions such as "Tamechika Ichidaiki", "Genji Ungenzuki", and "Yumihari-zuki Harushōei"<ref name=":2" />, and its themes became popular subjects for ukiyo-e prints and sugoroku board games<ref name=":0" />. {{Citation needed|date=2024th year 6月|本作の次に書いたのが『南総里見八犬伝』で、今日ではこちらの方が有名になっているが、当時は逆だった。|qq=}} === Modern Translations === * Translated by [[三田村信行]]: "Shinpen Yumihari-zuki" (Upper volume "Legend of the Hero" and Lower volume "The Evil Hand of the Demon King"), published by Poplar-sha, Heisei 18 (2006). (Children's edition.) * Translated by [[平岩弓枝]]: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki", published by Gakken Publishing (Gakken M Bunko), Heisei 14 (2002). (An edited version; first edition published by Gakken in 1981.) ** "Private Edition: Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" by Shinchosha, 2014/【Shincho Bunko】, 2017. * Translated by [[山田野理夫]]: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki", published by Kyōiku-sha Rekishi Shinsho (Upper and Lower volumes), originally in Shōwa 61 (1986). (Translation only; genealogical and ancillary sections omitted.) * Translated by [[高藤武馬]]: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" (Classical Japanese Literature Complete Works Vol. 27), published by Chikuma Shobō, Shōwa 35 (1960). (Translation only; extensive footnotes and non-essential commentary such as genealogies have been omitted in the reissued "Classical Japanese Literature" edition.) * Translated by Maruya Oke Hachi: "Complete Translation: Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki", published by Genkai Shobō, 2012. (Translation only; prefatory and supplementary critical sections are omitted.) ==== Collected Editions ==== * "Mishima Yukio Complete Works Vol.24 (Plays V)" (Shinchosha, April 25, 1975). ** Binding: Designed by [[杉山寧]], size Rokuroban, bound with cloth, with additional letters and initial performance data. * "Mishima Yukio Plays Complete Works, Lower Volume" (Shinchosha, September 10, 1990). ** Rokuroban size, two-column format, cloth-bound, presented as a two-volume set. * "Definitive Mishima Yukio Complete Works Vol.25: Plays 5" (Shinchosha, December 10, 2002). ** Binding details include special design, slipcover, and foil stamping. *** (Additional details and references omitted for brevity.) ==== Audio Materials ==== * LP Record "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki 'Upper Volume'" (Nippon Columbia, November 10, 1969). ** Features a reading by Mishima Yukio; gidayū performance by [[鶴澤燕三]] and [[野沢勝平]]; nagauta by [[杵屋栄左衛門]]; jacket design by [[横尾忠則]]. Comes with a booklet. ** Contains: "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki 'Upper Volume: Izu-koku Ōshima no Ba'". ** (The "Lower Volume" was not published.) ** Reissued in cassette and CD format in 1987 (Showa 62) <ref name="nikusei"/><ref name="rokuon"/>; the CD jacket differs, containing only Mishima's preface "Regarding the Recordization" <ref name="nikusei"/>. ** Also included as Disc 3 in "Definitive Mishima Yukio Complete Works Vol.41 Audio (CD)" (2004). === Film Adaptations === Two film adaptations were produced, one in 1914 (Taishō 3) and another in 1955 (Showa 30). * "Yumihari-zuki" (alternatively titled "Minamoto Tamechika") (Nikkatsu, May 1914) ** Directed by [[牧野省三]]; starring [[尾上松之助]]. ** Black-and-white silent film. * "Chinsetsu: Yumihari-zuki" (Toei, 1955) ** Released as a three-part series: "Part One: The Young Warriors of Chikushi" (September 20), "Part Two: The Fate of the White Shiranuin" (October 9), and "Final Part: The Conqueror of the South Seas" (October 17). ** Directed by [[丸根賛太郎]]; starring [[東千代之介]]. === Television Adaptations === * The serial puppet show "Shin Hakkenden" (1973–1975) ** One of the Hakkenshi, Inuzuka Nobu, travels to Ryukyu, where characters from "Yumihari-zuki"—such as Agō, Moyun, and Chūfukun—appear under the same names.<ref>{{Citation needed|その自由な創作ぶりを巡っては賛否両論で、『椿説里見八犬伝』と論評に書かれたこともあった|date=2024th year 1月|qq=}}</ref>. == Footnotes == {{Reflist|2|refs= <ref name="matsumura1952">{{cite book|lang=ja|ref={{SfnRef|松村|1952|qq=}}|author=松村明|title=むずかしい作品名の正しい読み方|journal=国文学 : 解釈と鑑賞|volume=17|number=6|dat=1952nd year 6月|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=X7qPEmZkJNsC&q=ちんぜい|page=35|qq=}}</ref> }} == References == {{参照方法|date=2020th year 11月|section=1|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|author=三島由紀夫|date=2002-12|title=決定版 三島由紀夫全集25巻 戯曲5|publisher=新潮社|isbn=978-4106425653|ref={{Harvid|戯曲25|2002|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|author=三島由紀夫|date=2003-10|title=決定版 三島由紀夫全集35巻 評論10|publisher=新潮社|isbn=978-4106425752|ref={{Harvid|35巻|2003|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|author=三島由紀夫|date=2004-09|title=決定版 三島由紀夫全集41巻 音声(CD)|publisher=新潮社|isbn=978-4106425813|ref={{Harvid|41巻|2004|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|editor1=佐藤秀明|editor2=井上隆史|editor3=山中剛史|date=2005-08|title=決定版 三島由紀夫全集42巻 年譜・書誌|publisher=新潮社|isbn=978-4106425820|ref={{Harvid|42巻|2005|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|author=安藤武|date=1998-09|title=三島由紀夫の生涯|publisher=夏目書房|isbn=978-4931391390|ref={{Harvid|生涯|1998|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|editor1=井上隆史|editor2=佐藤秀明|editor3=松本徹|date=2000-11|title=三島由紀夫事典|publisher=勉誠出版|isbn=978-4585060185|ref={{Harvid|事典|2000|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|editor1=長谷川泉|editor2=武田勝彦|date=1976-01|title=三島由紀夫事典|publisher=明治書院|id={{NCID|BN01686605|qq=}}|ref={{Harvid|旧事典|1976|qq=}}|qq=}} * {{Citation|lang=ja|others=松本徹監修|date=2010-10|title=別冊太陽 日本のこころ175――三島由紀夫|publisher=平凡社|isbn=978-4582921755|ref={{Harvid|太陽|2010|qq=}}|qq=}} == External Links == * [http://zeami.ci.sugiyama-u.ac.jp/dl/Yumihari/index.html "Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki" (Digital Library, Sugiyama University)] * 市川裕見子, "[https://hdl.handle.net/10241/00006844 A Memoir on Mishima Yukio’s Kabuki Works: Part V 'Chinsetsu Yumihari-zuki']," in ''Gaikokubungaku'' Issue 52, pp. 37–52, March 31, 2003, Utsunomiya University Foreign Literature Research Group, {{issn|0288-3309|qq=}}. {{三島由紀夫|qq=}}
== Bibliography == === Printed Editions === * Edited by [[和田万吉]]
}}
